name: OccField Manager (Complete Example)
description: Fully populated example showing what a comprehensive knowledge base looks like for an HQMC OccField Manager billet.

entries.title: MOS Manual Change Process
entries.content:
## MOS Manual Change Process (MCO 1200.17)

This is the single most complex and high-visibility process you own. A MOS Manual change affects every Marine in the OccField, every school seat allocation, and every T/O across the force. Get this wrong and you will spend months cleaning up the damage. Get it right and it becomes the foundation of healthy force management for years.

### Step 1: Initiation (Weeks 1-4)

1. **Identify the requirement.** Changes originate from three sources: (a) fleet feedback through OccField symposiums or unit requests, (b) TECOM identifying training pipeline misalignments, or (c) your own analysis of MOS health data showing structural problems.
2. **Draft the proposed change.** Use the MCO 1200.17 change template (available on the PP&O SharePoint under OccField Manager Resources). The draft must include: the specific MOS(s) affected, proposed changes to title/description/prerequisites/PMOS-to-AMOS relationships, justification narrative, and impact assessment.
3. **Informal pre-coordination.** Before entering the formal staffing cycle, send the draft to three key stakeholders for a sanity check: your M&RA monitor (will this create assignment problems?), TECOM training POC (can the schoolhouse support this?), and your Branch Chief (does this align with PP&O priorities?). **This step is not optional.** Skipping it is the number one reason changes get rejected later.

### Step 2: Formal Staffing (Weeks 5-16)

1. **Route through PP&O internal review.** Your Branch Chief reviews and endorses, then it goes to DirOps for signature. DirOps may ask clarifying questions or request additional data. **Typical delay: 1-2 weeks** if you haven't pre-briefed DirOps.
2. **M&RA coordination.** The change package goes to M&RA (MPP-40) for formal review. They assess manning impact, assignment feasibility, and consistency with other pending changes. **Typical delay: 2-4 weeks.** M&RA will push back hard if the change creates unfillable billets or breaks their assignment algorithms.
3. **TECOM coordination.** Simultaneous with M&RA, TECOM reviews training pipeline impacts. They need to confirm they can adjust curriculum, instructor staffing, and throughput to support the change. **Typical delay: 3-6 weeks** for significant changes.
4. **CMC Staff review.** For major changes (new MOS creation, MOS deletion, significant prerequisite changes), the package routes through the CMC staff for final approval. **Typical delay: 4-8 weeks.**

### Step 3: Publication (Weeks 17-24)

1. **Final editing and formatting.** Once all concurrences are received, the approved change goes to the Publications office for formatting and incorporation into the next MOS Manual update.
2. **Distribution.** The updated manual is published and distributed via the Marine Corps Publications Electronic Library (MCPEL). Effective dates are typically 60-90 days after publication to allow units to adjust.

### Common Delays and How to Avoid Them

- **Incomplete impact assessments** cause the most rejections. Always include specific numbers: how many Marines affected, how many billets change, what the training pipeline delta is.
- **Lack of fleet input** makes reviewers skeptical. Include at least two endorsements from operational commands (MEF or MEB level preferred).
- **Timing conflicts** with POM cycles or other pending changes can freeze your submission. Check the PP&O change tracker before initiating.

### Approval Authorities

- **Minor changes** (title, description wording): DirOps, PP&O
- **Moderate changes** (prerequisite adjustments, PMOS-AMOS relationships): DC PP&O
- **Major changes** (new MOS, MOS deletion, restructure): CMC via DC PP&O

entries.title: Manning Request and Billet Change Procedures
entries.content:
## Manning Request and Billet Change Procedures

Manning and billet management are the bread and butter of OccField management. Commands will constantly request changes -- more billets, different grade structures, new MOSs on their T/O. Your job is to balance operational need against total force capacity while keeping the T/O healthy and sustainable.

### How Billet Change Requests Originate

1. **Unit-initiated requests.** A commanding officer submits a T/O Change Request (TOCR) through the chain of command. The request routes through the respective MEF or supporting establishment G-1, then to M&RA, and eventually lands on your desk for OccField manager concurrence.
2. **Force structure reviews.** During periodic force structure reviews (FSR), higher headquarters may direct billet additions, deletions, or grade changes based on capability assessments. These are typically non-negotiable but you should shape the implementation.
3. **OccField manager-initiated.** When MOS health data shows structural problems -- chronic overages at one grade, persistent shortages at another -- you can proactively propose billet restructuring through PP&O.

### T/O Change Request Process

1. **Receipt and review.** When a TOCR arrives, verify it is complete: justification narrative, specific billet details (MOS, grade, unit, T/O line number), and endorsements from the chain of command.
2. **OccField impact analysis.** Assess how the change affects total force inventory. Key questions: Does this create a billet we can't fill? Does it shift grade structure in a way that hurts career progression? Does it duplicate a capability already on the T/O?
3. **Coordination.** Coordinate with the M&RA monitor (assignment feasibility), TECOM (training pipeline impact if new MOS), and the requesting command's G-1 (clarifications).
4. **Recommendation.** Draft your concurrence or non-concurrence memo. Include specific data: current fill rates for the MOS/grade, projected inventory, and any conditions on your concurrence.
5. **Routing.** Your recommendation goes through the Branch Chief to DirOps for the PP&O position, then to the T/O Review Board for final adjudication.

### Approval Authority by Scope

- **1-5 billets, same unit:** T/O Review Board (quarterly)
- **6-20 billets or cross-unit:** DC PP&O approval required
- **20+ billets or new unit activation:** CMC-level decision, typically tied to force structure review

### Realistic Timelines

- **Simple billet swap** (same MOS, different grade): 2-3 months
- **New billet addition** with funded manpower: 4-6 months
- **Billet addition requiring POM funding**: 12-18 months minimum (must make the next POM cycle)
- **Unit activation or major restructure**: 18-24 months

### Common Mistakes

- **Concurring without checking inventory.** Never approve a billet addition if the MOS is already below 90% fill. You are creating a hollow billet that makes your metrics worse.
- **Ignoring grade structure.** A request for a GySgt billet in an MOS where every GySgt slot is already filled means this billet will sit empty for years. Push back with data.
- **Not tracking cumulative impact.** Individual TOCRs look reasonable in isolation. Track them in aggregate -- ten small requests can fundamentally reshape your OccField's grade structure.

entries.title: TECOM School Seat and Curriculum Coordination
entries.content:
## TECOM School Seat and Curriculum Coordination

The training pipeline is the lifeblood of OccField health. If Marines cannot get school seats, MOSs go unfilled regardless of how many billets exist. Your relationship with TECOM and the schoolhouse directly determines whether the pipeline flows or chokes.

### School Seat Allocation Process

1. **Annual quota establishment.** Each fiscal year, TECOM establishes school seat quotas for every formal MOS-producing course. Quotas are based on: (a) historical throughput, (b) projected requirements from T/O, (c) schoolhouse instructor and facility capacity.
2. **Quota negotiation.** As OccField manager, you provide TECOM with your projected requirements based on inventory data and anticipated losses (EAS, lateral moves, retirement). Submit your requirements during the annual Training Input Plan (TIP) cycle, typically due in **March** for the following fiscal year.
3. **Seat allocation.** TECOM allocates seats across the MEFs and supporting establishment based on fill priorities. You do not directly assign seats to units, but you heavily influence the allocation formula through your requirement inputs.
4. **Mid-year adjustments.** If throughput problems arise (instructor shortages, facility issues, high attrition), TECOM may reduce quotas mid-year. When this happens, coordinate immediately with M&RA to adjust assignment projections.

### Coordination Rhythm

- **Monthly:** Training Pipeline Sync with TECOM Training Pipeline Manager. Review current course throughput, attrition rates, upcoming class start dates, and any instructor staffing issues.
- **Quarterly:** Formal Curriculum Review Board. Review course learning objectives, student feedback, fleet feedback on graduate quality, and any proposed curriculum changes.
- **Annually:** Training Input Plan submission and face-to-face planning conference at Quantico.

### Curriculum Review Process

1. **Fleet feedback collection.** Gather input from operational commands on graduate quality. Are new joins arriving with the right skills? What gaps exist? Use the OccField symposium and direct liaison with unit SNCOs.
2. **Review with TECOM.** Present fleet feedback at the Curriculum Review Board. Propose specific changes to learning objectives, course length, or practical application requirements.
3. **Implementation timeline.** Curriculum changes typically take 6-12 months to implement. Major changes (course length, new POI) require formal approval from CG TECOM. Minor changes (updated references, adjusted practical exercises) can be implemented at the schoolhouse level.

### Key Contacts and Systems

- **TECOM Training Pipeline Manager:** Primary POC for all school seat issues. Located at Quantico. Schedule quarterly face-to-face visits.
- **Schoolhouse Director / OIC:** Direct liaison for day-to-day training issues. Maintain a monthly phone call minimum.
- **Training and Education Command Information System (TECMIS):** System of record for school seat data. Request read access through TECOM.

### Gotchas

- **Instructor staffing drives everything.** The number one limiter on school seat capacity is instructor billets. If instructor billets are unfilled, class sizes shrink regardless of student demand. Monitor instructor fill rates as closely as student throughput.
- **Attrition rates are your early warning.** If course attrition spikes above 15%, something is wrong -- screening criteria, instructor quality, or curriculum mismatch. Investigate immediately; don't wait for the quarterly review.

entries.title: OccField Specialist Symposium / Conference Planning
entries.content:
## OccField Specialist Symposium / Conference Planning

The OccField symposium is your single best opportunity to gather fleet feedback, build consensus on OccField direction, and demonstrate to senior leadership that you are actively managing the field. It is also a massive logistics undertaking that will consume weeks of your time if you don't start early enough.

### Planning Timeline (Start T-6 Months)

**T-6 months:**
1. Determine dates and venue. The National Conference Center at Quantico is the default, but if attendance exceeds 80 you may need the Clubs or an off-base venue. Reserve the venue immediately -- they book up fast, especially in spring.
2. Draft the initial concept paper. Include: purpose, scope, proposed attendees (by billet, not name), draft agenda topics, and estimated cost. Route through Branch Chief for approval.
3. Begin budget coordination. Submit a funding request through PP&O comptroller. Typical costs: $15K-$25K for a 3-day symposium (room rental, A/V, materials, per diem for non-local attendees). Travel costs are borne by sending commands.

**T-4 months:**
1. Finalize attendee list and send invitations. Use the official PP&O letterhead. Include: dates, location, agenda overview, travel/billeting guidance, and uniform requirements.
2. Identify guest speakers. Invite the DC PP&O or DirOps for opening remarks. A TECOM representative and M&RA monitor should present as well. Fleet SNCOs as panel members add credibility.
3. Begin developing working group agendas. Each working group should address a specific OccField issue with a defined deliverable (recommendation, data requirement, action item).

**T-2 months:**
1. Collect RSVPs and resolve travel issues. Some commands will resist sending personnel. Have your Branch Chief engage their leadership if necessary.
2. Finalize agenda and distribute read-ahead materials. Include current MOS health data, pending policy changes, and specific questions you want addressed.
3. Coordinate A/V, printing, and administrative support.

**T-1 week:**
1. Confirm all logistics: room setup, A/V test, printed materials, name tents, sign-in roster.
2. Pre-brief guest speakers on their time allocation and key themes.
3. Assign note-takers for each session.

### Execution

- **Day 1:** Opening remarks, MOS health overview, keynote presentations.
- **Day 2:** Working groups on specific topics (training pipeline, retention, career progression, emerging requirements).
- **Day 3:** Working group outbriefs, consensus building, closing remarks, action item assignment.

### After-Action

1. Publish the after-action report within 30 days. Include all working group recommendations, action items with owners and due dates, and key takeaways.
2. Brief DirOps on symposium outcomes and recommended actions.
3. Track action items to completion. The symposium is meaningless if recommendations die on the vine.

### Lessons From Previous Symposiums

- **Keep working groups small (8-12 people).** Larger groups devolve into unfocused discussion.
- **Assign a facilitator to each group** who is not the OccField manager. You should float between groups.
- **Record everything.** Symposium recommendations become the justification for future MOS Manual changes and POM submissions.

entries.title: Quarterly MOS Health Reporting
entries.content:
## Quarterly MOS Health Reporting

The quarterly MOS Health Report is your primary tool for keeping leadership informed and for driving decisions about your OccField. If you do this well, problems get addressed before they become crises. If you do this poorly, you will spend your time reacting instead of managing.

### Data Sources

1. **Total Force Data Warehouse (TFDW).** Primary source for inventory data: how many Marines are in each MOS by grade. Access through the TFDW web portal. **Critical caveat: TFDW data lags by 60-90 days.** Never brief TFDW numbers alone as current inventory.
2. **Marine Corps Total Force System (MCTFS).** Better for near-real-time snapshots of individual Marine status. Use for cross-referencing when TFDW numbers seem off.
3. **M&RA Monitor's Tracking Spreadsheet.** Your monitor at MPP-40 maintains a live Excel file with current fill rates, pending PCS orders, and projected gains/losses. **This is your most accurate source** for current manning posture. Get a copy weekly.
4. **TECOM Training Pipeline Reports.** Monthly reports on student throughput, attrition, and graduation projections. These feed your supply-side analysis.
5. **Retention Data.** MMEA provides reenlistment and retention data by MOS. Quarterly updates.

### Report Format

The standard format for the DirOps brief includes:

1. **Executive Summary.** One slide. Overall OccField health (Green/Amber/Red by MOS), top 3 issues, recommended actions.
2. **Inventory vs. Requirement.** Table showing T/O requirement, current inventory, fill percentage, and trend (improving/stable/declining) for each MOS by grade.
3. **Pipeline Status.** Training throughput for the quarter, attrition rates, projected graduates for next two quarters.
4. **Retention Analysis.** First-term and career reenlistment rates by MOS. Compare to Service-wide averages. Flag any MOS with retention below 80% of target.
5. **Issues and Actions.** Active issues with status, owner, and timeline. New issues identified this quarter.

### Process

**Week 1 (T-21 days):** Pull data from all sources. Begin building the slide deck.
**Week 2 (T-14 days):** Complete analysis. Identify key trends and issues. Draft recommendations.
**Week 3 (T-7 days):** Pre-brief Branch Chief. Incorporate feedback. Finalize slides.
**Brief day:** Present to DirOps. Typical format is 15-minute brief with 10 minutes for Q&A.

### Distribution

After the brief, distribute the report to:
- Branch Chief and DirOps (already briefed)
- M&RA monitor (their data feeds the report; they need to see your analysis)
- TECOM training POC (pipeline-related findings)
- OccField community (via official distribution list, sanitized version without internal recommendations)

### Tips

- **Tell a story, not just numbers.** DirOps sees dozens of data briefs. Make yours memorable by connecting data to operational impact.
- **Always have a recommendation.** Never brief a problem without a proposed solution.
- **Track trends, not just snapshots.** A 92% fill rate that was 97% last quarter is more alarming than an 85% fill rate that was 80% last quarter.

entries.title: Career Path Development and PME Coordination
entries.content:
## Career Path Development and PME Coordination

Career path management is one of the less visible but most impactful things you do. A well-designed career path attracts and retains talent. A confusing or stagnant one drives Marines to lateral move or separate. You are the architect of how Marines progress through your OccField from PFC to MGySgt.

### Career Path Components

1. **MOS Progression.** Define the expected progression from entry-level PMOS through advanced MOSs and any AMOS qualifications. Document which lateral moves are encouraged, which are discouraged, and why.
2. **Key Billets.** Identify the billets at each grade that are considered career-enhancing. Not all billets are equal -- a squad leader at a line company carries more weight than the same grade at a garrison support unit. Document this so Marines and monitors can make informed decisions.
3. **PME Requirements.** Map Professional Military Education requirements to career milestones. Sergeants need the Sergeants Course before the next promotion board. SNCOs need the Advanced Course. Officers need equivalent PME gating.
4. **Certifications and Additional Training.** Identify any civilian certifications, joint qualifications, or additional military courses that enhance career progression. Note which are mandatory vs. recommended.

### Coordination with Education Command

- **Quarterly sync** with Marine Corps University (MCU) / Education Command on PME seat allocation for your OccField. PME seats are competitive and limited.
- **Annual input** to the Commandant's PME guidance on any OccField-specific requirements.
- **Coordination on MOS-specific advanced courses** (e.g., Expeditionary Warfare School for infantry, but equivalent for your field's advanced schooling).

### Career Path Publication

The official career path is published in the MOS Roadmap, available on the Marine Corps Center for Lessons Learned (MCCLL) and distributed through the OccField community. Update the roadmap annually during the MOS Manual review cycle.

### Common Issues

- **Grade pyramiding.** If too many senior billets exist relative to junior billets, you get bottlenecks at the mid-grade level. Marines cannot promote because there are no billets above them to move into. Monitor grade distribution quarterly.
- **Lateral move hemorrhaging.** If Marines are laterally moving out of your OccField at high rates, the career path may be perceived as unattractive. Survey departing Marines to understand why.
- **PME completion rates.** If Marines in your OccField have low PME completion rates, investigate whether it is a seat allocation issue or a command priority issue. Both require different solutions.

### Your Role

You do not directly control assignments or promotions. But you shape the environment: which MOSs exist, what the prerequisites are, which billets are on the T/O, and how the career path is communicated. Use the symposium, the MOS Manual review, and your relationship with the monitor to keep the career path healthy and aligned with both Marine needs and institutional requirements.

entries.title: Interservice Coordination for Joint Billets
entries.content:
## Interservice Coordination for Joint Billets

Joint billets are a small but important part of your OccField portfolio. They consume your best mid-grade and senior Marines, affect retention calculations, and require coordination with organizations that do not operate on Marine Corps timelines or processes.

### Joint Billet Inventory

Maintain a running list of all joint billets that require Marines from your OccField. Sources include:
- **Joint Manning Document (JMD)** for combatant commands (CENTCOM, INDOPACOM, EUCOM, etc.)
- **Defense agency billets** (DIA, NSA, CYBERCOM)
- **NATO and coalition billets**
- **Joint Staff billets** at the Pentagon

For each billet, track: location, grade, MOS, current fill status, rotation date, and the owning organization's POC.

### Coordination Process

1. **Annual joint billet review.** Each year, the Joint Manpower Program office coordinates a review of joint billet requirements. You provide input on which billets your OccField can fill, which are unfillable (and why), and any proposed changes.
2. **Fill coordination.** Joint billet fills are coordinated through M&RA (MMEA for enlisted, MMOA for officers). Your role is to concur on the MOS appropriateness of the fill, not the specific Marine selected.
3. **JPME requirements.** Marines filling joint billets at O-4 and above typically need Joint Professional Military Education Phase I completion. Ensure your career path documentation reflects this requirement.

### Sister Service Coordination

- **Army Signal Branch:** The Army equivalent of the Marine Corps 06XX OccField. Their OccField manager at HRC (Fort Knox) is the primary counterpart. Annual coordination meeting typically held in September at the Joint Communications Support Forum.
- **Navy Information Warfare Community:** Coordinate on SIGINT and EW-related joint billets and interoperability requirements.
- **Air Force Cyber Operations:** Increasingly relevant for joint cyber billets and training standardization.

### Interservice Transfer

Occasionally, Marines seek interservice transfer into or out of your OccField. The process is governed by DoD Directive 1300.4. Your role is limited to concurrence on MOS equivalency for inbound transfers and providing retention impact assessments for outbound transfers.

### Key Considerations

- **Joint billets consume your best talent.** These billets typically require experienced SNCOs and mid-grade officers. Account for this in your manning projections.
- **Return on investment.** Marines returning from joint assignments bring valuable joint experience but may need refresher training on USMC-specific systems and procedures. Coordinate with TECOM on reintegration training if applicable.
- **Don't let joint billets go unfilled.** Unfilled joint billets generate negative attention at the SECDEF level. If you cannot fill a billet, formally request it be re-coded to a different MOS or deleted from the JMD. Do not leave it vacant and hope nobody notices.

entries.title: FY25 MOS Restructure: Merge 0621/0623 into 0671
entries.content:
## Decision: Merge MOS 0621 (Field Radio Operator) and MOS 0623 (MAGTF Planner, Comm) into MOS 0671 (Data Systems Administrator)

### Decision Date
15 September 2025

### What Was Decided

The Marine Corps will consolidate MOS 0621 (Field Radio Operator) and MOS 0623 (MAGTF Planner, Communications) into an expanded MOS 0671 (Data Systems Administrator). Effective date: 1 October 2026, with a 24-month transition window for existing Marines in the legacy MOSs.

### Why This Decision Was Made

1. **Converging skill sets.** Modern communications systems increasingly require both traditional radio operation and data networking skills. The distinction between a "radio operator" and a "data systems administrator" has become artificial at the tactical level.
2. **Inventory pressure.** MOS 0621 has been chronically overstaffed at the E-4/E-5 level (112% fill) while 0671 has been persistently understaffed (78% fill). Merging the MOSs allows the combined inventory to better match total requirements.
3. **Training efficiency.** Maintaining three separate courses with overlapping content was consuming instructor billets that could be consolidated. TECOM estimated a savings of 8 instructor billets.
4. **Fleet feedback.** The FY24 OccField symposium produced a unanimous recommendation from field-grade working group to consolidate these MOSs. Operational commanders reported no meaningful distinction in how they employed 0621s and 0623s.

### Alternatives Considered

1. **Status quo.** Rejected because inventory imbalances and training inefficiencies would persist.
2. **Merge 0621 into 0623 only.** Rejected because 0623 was too narrow a platform to absorb 0621 skills; the combined MOS needed to be broader.
3. **Create a new MOS (0625).** Rejected because creating a new MOS from scratch introduces more administrative burden than expanding an existing one. Also, 0671 already had the closest training pipeline to support the merger.
4. **Phased approach: merge 0621/0623 first, evaluate, then consider 0671.** Rejected as adding unnecessary delay. The analysis clearly supported a single consolidated MOS.

### Implementation Plan

- **Phase 1 (Oct 2025 - Mar 2026):** TECOM develops unified curriculum. Existing 0671 course expands to include radio and planning modules.
- **Phase 2 (Apr - Sep 2026):** Last classes graduate under legacy MOSs. New unified course begins accepting students.
- **Phase 3 (Oct 2026 - Sep 2028):** Transition window. Existing 0621/0623 Marines reclassify to 0671 via MOS Administrative Action (no course required for Marines with 2+ years in the legacy MOS).

### Impact

- **Training pipeline:** Net reduction of 3 weeks in combined training time. Savings of 8 instructor billets.
- **Manning:** Projected improvement from 78% combined fill to 94% within 18 months of implementation.
- **Career progression:** Combined MOS creates a healthier grade pyramid with more opportunities at the E-6/E-7 level.

### Reversibility

**Not readily reversible.** Once the legacy MOSs are deleted from the Manual and the training pipeline is consolidated, re-separating them would require a new MOS creation process (12-18 months minimum). This decision should be treated as permanent.

meta.alternatives: Status quo (rejected: persistent imbalances); Merge 0621 into 0623 only (rejected: too narrow); Create new MOS 0625 (rejected: unnecessary admin burden); Phased approach (rejected: unnecessary delay)
meta.outcome: Merger approved by CMC staff. Implementation in three phases through Sep 2028. Training pipeline consolidation underway.

entries.title: Training Pipeline Adjustment: Reduced Course Length for 0612
entries.content:
## Decision: Reduce MOS 0612 (Tactical Switching Operator) Course from 12 Weeks to 9 Weeks

### Decision Date
5 January 2026

### What Was Decided

The formal MOS-producing course for 0612 (Tactical Switching Operator) was reduced from 12 weeks to 9 weeks, effective with Class 26-004 (start date March 2026). Three weeks of legacy switching platform instruction were removed and replaced with a condensed module focused on the replacement system.

### Why This Decision Was Made

1. **Equipment fielding.** The legacy AN/TTC-56 switching system is being replaced by the modernized IP-based system across all operating forces. Spending 3 weeks training Marines on equipment they will never see in the fleet was wasteful.
2. **Pipeline throughput.** The 0612 MOS had a 23% school seat shortage (see Active Issue: tpl_029). Reducing course length by 3 weeks allows an additional class cycle per fiscal year, increasing annual throughput by approximately 40 graduates.
3. **TECOM recommendation.** The schoolhouse OIC presented data showing that the 3 weeks of legacy system training had a near-zero relevance to fleet duties based on a survey of 0612 Marines at the MEFs.

### Risk Assessment

- **Risk: Graduates arrive less technically proficient.** Mitigated by the fact that removed content was legacy-specific. The replacement system is operationally simpler. Fleet feedback will be monitored for 12 months post-implementation.
- **Risk: Marines assigned to units still operating legacy equipment.** Low probability -- fielding plan shows less than 5% of units still on legacy systems by mid-2026. Those units can provide OJT.
- **Risk: Instructor reallocation disruption.** Three instructor billets freed by the course reduction will be reassigned to the 0671 merged course (see Decision: tpl_008). Coordination with TECOM complete.

### Metrics for Success

- Graduate quality: MOS-producing course pass rate remains above 90%
- Fleet satisfaction: Unit commander feedback on graduate readiness remains at "satisfactory" or above
- Throughput: Annual graduate count increases by 35-45 Marines

### Review Timeline

This decision will be formally reviewed at the Q4 FY26 Curriculum Review Board (July 2026) based on the first two graduating classes under the new POI.

meta.alternatives: Maintain 12-week course (rejected: wasted training time); Remove 3 weeks and add cyber content (rejected: scope creep, insufficient instructor qualification)
meta.outcome: Course reduced to 9 weeks. First class under new POI starts March 2026.

entries.title: School Seat Allocation: Priority to I MEF over II MEF
entries.content:
## Decision: FY26 School Seat Priority Allocation Favoring I MEF

### Decision Date
12 November 2025

### What Was Decided

For FY26 Q2-Q4 school seat allocation for MOS 0612 and 0631 courses, I MEF receives 60% of available seats and II MEF receives 40%. The standard split is 50/50. This temporary reallocation applies for three quarters only (Jan-Sep 2026).

### Why This Decision Was Made

1. **Deployment cycle alignment.** I MEF has a major deployment rotation beginning in August 2026. Their deploying battalions are currently at 72% fill for 0612 and 68% for 0631. Deploying below 80% fill creates unacceptable risk to mission.
2. **II MEF post-deployment recovery.** II MEF units recently returned from deployment and are in a reconstitution phase. Their fill rates are lower overall but do not face the same deployment timeline pressure.
3. **M&RA concurrence.** The monitor confirmed that the reallocation aligns with the assignment plan and will not create downstream problems for II MEF's next deployment cycle (projected FY27 Q2).

### Coordination

- **I MEF G-1:** Concurred. Provided specific unit fill data supporting the request.
- **II MEF G-1:** Accepted with the understanding that the standard 50/50 split resumes in FY27 Q1 and that II MEF receives priority consideration if additional seats become available mid-year.
- **TECOM:** Confirmed seat availability supports the reallocation without impacting total throughput.
- **M&RA Monitor:** Concurred. Assignment projections adjusted.

### Conditions

- **This is a temporary measure.** Standard allocation formula resumes FY27 Q1.
- **If II MEF deployment timeline accelerates,** this allocation will be reassessed immediately.
- **Quarterly review** at each MOS Health brief to confirm the reallocation is achieving desired fill rates.

### Lessons for Future Allocations

This decision required extensive pre-coordination to avoid being perceived as favoring one MEF over another. The key to gaining II MEF G-1 concurrence was presenting clear deployment timeline data and guaranteeing the reversion to standard allocation. Document everything -- MEF G-1s have long memories.

meta.alternatives: Maintain 50/50 split (rejected: I MEF deploys below threshold); Request additional seats from TECOM (rejected: no instructor capacity); Shift seats from III MEF (rejected: Pacific posture requirements)
meta.outcome: I MEF 60/40 priority allocation for 3 quarters. II MEF accepted with reversion guarantee.

entries.title: Monitor Coordination: Shift from Quarterly to Monthly Syncs
entries.content:
## Decision: Increase M&RA Monitor Coordination from Quarterly to Monthly

### Decision Date
20 August 2025

### What Was Decided

The standing coordination meeting between the OccField Manager and the M&RA monitor (MPP-40) shifted from quarterly to monthly, effective September 2025. The meeting is a 60-minute video call on the second Tuesday of each month.

### What Triggered the Change

During the FY25 Q3 MOS Health brief, DirOps flagged that several manning issues had festered for months without resolution. Investigation revealed that the quarterly coordination cadence was too slow to catch and resolve problems before they compounded. Specifically:

1. **A PCS misalignment** sent 12 Marines to units that didn't have matching billets. By the time it was discovered at the quarterly meeting, the Marines were already in transit. Cost: 3 months of lost productivity and significant unit frustration.
2. **A retention shortfall** in MOS 0631 went unaddressed for 4 months because the data wasn't reviewed until the next quarterly sync. By then, the window for retention bonuses had closed for FY25.
3. **School seat cancellations** at TECOM were not communicated to the monitor for 6 weeks, causing downstream assignment mismatches.

### Format

- **Duration:** 60 minutes, second Tuesday of each month, 1000-1100 EST
- **Agenda:** (1) Current inventory snapshot, (2) Pending PCS actions, (3) School seat pipeline status, (4) Retention data review, (5) Open issues
- **Deliverable:** Both parties maintain a shared running action log (OneNote notebook in the PP&O Teams channel)

### Outcome

Since shifting to monthly coordination:
- PCS misalignment incidents dropped from 3-4 per quarter to zero
- Open issues average resolution time decreased from 47 days to 12 days
- The monitor relationship has significantly strengthened -- they now proactively flag issues before they appear in data

### Recommendation for Successor

**Do not revert to quarterly.** The monthly cadence is essential. If your monitor suggests quarterly is sufficient, politely decline. The investment of 12 hours per year in monthly syncs saves hundreds of hours in problem remediation.

meta.alternatives: Maintain quarterly cadence (rejected: too slow to catch issues); Weekly calls (considered but rejected as excessive given current workload); Bi-weekly calls (acceptable but monthly was sufficient)
meta.outcome: Monthly syncs implemented. Measurable improvement in issue resolution time and PCS accuracy.

entries.title: M&RA Monitor, MPP-40
entries.content:
## M&RA Monitor, MPP-40 — Primary Manning Coordination

This is the single most important external relationship you maintain. The monitor controls PCS orders for every Marine in your OccField. They decide who goes where, when. If you have a strong relationship with your monitor, manning problems get solved quickly. If you don't, they still get solved -- just not in your favor.

### Role and Responsibilities

The monitor at MPP-40 (Manpower Plans and Policy, Enlisted Assignments) is responsible for:
- **Individual Marine assignments** within your OccField. They match Marines to billets based on T/O requirements, deployment schedules, and individual Marine preferences (in that order).
- **PCS order generation** and coordination with receiving commands.
- **Fill priority management.** When demand exceeds supply (which is always), the monitor decides which units get filled first. Your input heavily influences these priorities.
- **Inventory tracking.** The monitor maintains the most current inventory data for your OccField, updated daily.

### Coordination Rhythm

- **Monthly sync:** Second Tuesday, 1000-1100 EST (see Decision: tpl_011). Covers inventory, pending PCS, school seat pipeline, retention, open issues.
- **Ad hoc coordination:** Expect 2-3 phone calls or emails per week on specific assignment questions. Respond within 24 hours -- the monitor has deadlines.
- **Transfer season (Apr-Jun):** Coordination intensity doubles. Expect daily contact during peak PCS season.

### How to Work Effectively with the Monitor

1. **Bring data, not opinions.** When you need a fill priority changed, show them the numbers: current fill rate, deployment timeline, operational impact.
2. **Understand their constraints.** The monitor manages multiple MOSs and has finite Marines to distribute. Your OccField is not their only priority. Acknowledge this.
3. **Don't go around them.** If you escalate to M&RA leadership without first trying to resolve through the monitor, you will damage the relationship for years. Escalate only as a last resort and always tell the monitor first.
4. **Provide early warning.** If you know a retention problem is coming, a training pipeline issue is developing, or a T/O change is in the works, tell the monitor before it shows up in their data. No surprises.
5. **Face time matters.** Visit M&RA at Arlington at least once per quarter. A 30-minute in-person conversation builds more trust than a dozen emails.

### What They Need From You

- Accurate, timely T/O data (which billets exist, which are funded)
- Early notice of MOS Manual changes that affect assignments
- Concurrence or non-concurrence on fill priorities within 48 hours of request
- Feedback on how their assignments are working at the fleet level

meta.relationship: Primary coordination for all 06XX Manning. Call this person first for any assignment or inventory question. They control PCS orders and have final say on fill priorities. Build a strong working relationship — they can make or break your manning posture.

entries.title: TECOM Training Pipeline POC
entries.content:
## TECOM Training Pipeline Manager — Training Pipeline Coordination

Your TECOM counterpart manages the supply side of your OccField equation. They control school seat capacity, curriculum content, instructor staffing, and student throughput. When the training pipeline is healthy, manning problems are manageable. When it chokes, everything downstream breaks within 6-12 months.

### Role and Responsibilities

The Training Pipeline Manager at TECOM (Training and Education Command) is responsible for:
- **School seat allocation** across all formal courses producing Marines for your OccField
- **Curriculum management** including the Program of Instruction (POI), learning objectives, and assessment standards
- **Instructor staffing** oversight -- ensuring schoolhouses have qualified instructors in sufficient quantity
- **Attrition tracking** and analysis of student drops, recycles, and academic failures
- **Coordination with schoolhouse OICs** on day-to-day training execution

### Coordination Rhythm

- **Monthly:** Training Pipeline Sync (phone/VTC). Review throughput data, attrition trends, upcoming class schedules, and any emerging issues.
- **Quarterly:** Curriculum Review Board (in-person at Quantico preferred). Formal review of course content against fleet requirements. Bring fleet feedback.
- **Annually:** Training Input Plan coordination (Jan-Mar). Submit your projected training requirements for the next fiscal year.

### Relationship Management

1. **Visit the schoolhouse at least once per quarter.** Walk the training areas, talk to instructors, observe practical exercises. You cannot effectively manage a training pipeline from behind a desk at the Pentagon.
2. **Instructor staffing is their biggest headache.** When instructor billets go unfilled, class sizes shrink and throughput drops. Support their instructor staffing requests through your M&RA coordination.
3. **Fleet feedback is your currency.** TECOM values fleet input above all else. When you bring specific, constructive feedback from operational commands about graduate quality, they listen and act. Vague complaints are ignored.
4. **Respect the POI change process.** Curriculum changes require formal approval. Don't ask for "quick changes" to course content -- it undermines the quality assurance process and puts instructors in an impossible position.

### What They Need From You

- Accurate training requirement projections (how many Marines you need trained per year)
- Fleet feedback on graduate quality (specific, actionable, documented)
- Support for instructor staffing requests when they coordinate through PP&O
- Early warning on MOS Manual changes that affect training pipeline structure
- Attendance at Curriculum Review Boards (your absence signals you don't care about training quality)

meta.relationship: Manages school seat allocation, curriculum reviews, and instructor staffing. Contact when course throughput drops or curriculum needs updating. Schedule face-to-face at least once per quarter.

entries.title: DC I OccField Analyst
entries.content:
## DC I OccField Analyst — Intelligence Community Coordination

The DC I (Director of Intelligence) OccField Analyst provides the intelligence community perspective on communications and data systems requirements. This relationship is essential when your OccField intersects with SIGINT, Electronic Warfare, or intelligence systems -- which happens more frequently than you might expect.

### Role and Responsibilities

- **Cross-OccField requirements analysis** where 06XX (Communications) and 26XX (Intelligence) capabilities overlap
- **SIGINT/EW billet requirements** that touch both OccFields
- **Emerging technology assessments** that impact both communications and intelligence training
- **Joint and NSA billet coordination** for positions requiring both 06XX and intelligence community qualifications

### Coordination Rhythm

- **Monthly:** Standing phone call, typically third Thursday. Discuss any cross-OccField issues, pending billet changes that affect both fields, and upcoming policy changes.
- **As needed:** They will reach out when DC I is staffing something that touches your equities. Expect 1-2 ad hoc coordination requests per month.

### When to Engage This Contact

1. **Any MOS Manual change** that involves MOSs with SIGINT, EW, or cyber components
2. **Joint billet reviews** for positions at NSA, CYBERCOM, or DIA that require 06XX MOSs
3. **Training pipeline changes** that affect cross-OccField courses or shared training resources
4. **Force Design initiatives** that merge or realign capabilities between 06XX and 26XX

### Relationship Dynamics

The intelligence community operates with different classification constraints. Some of what the DC I analyst works on cannot be discussed at the UNCLASS level. If they are vague about the rationale for a request, it may be because they cannot share the full picture. Accept this and work within the constraints.

Conversely, they rely on you for ground-truth manning data that informs their requirements analysis. Be responsive and transparent with your data -- it builds trust and ensures their requirements are grounded in reality rather than aspirational numbers.

meta.relationship: Provides intelligence community perspective on 06XX requirements. Coordinates SIGINT/EW billet requirements. Key contact for any 26XX-related cross-OccField issues.

entries.title: Sister Service Liaison (Army Signal)
entries.content:
## Army Signal Branch OccField Manager — Interservice Coordination

The Army Signal Branch equivalent of your billet sits at Human Resources Command (HRC) in Fort Knox, Kentucky. This is a less frequent coordination touchpoint but an important one for joint billet management, training interoperability, and staying aware of how the largest service handles the same capability domain.

### Coordination Rhythm

- **Quarterly:** Phone call to review joint billet status and any emerging interoperability issues.
- **Annually:** Joint Communications Support Forum (typically September). This is the primary face-to-face venue. Attend if at all possible.
- **Ad hoc:** Coordinate when joint billet reviews surface or when interservice training opportunities arise.

### Areas of Coordination

1. **Joint billet management.** Several combatant command billets can be filled by either Army Signal or Marine 06XX Marines. Coordinate on fill priorities and rotation schedules to avoid gaps.
2. **Training interoperability.** The Army Signal School at Fort Eisenhower (formerly Fort Gordon) occasionally offers seats to Marine students for specialized courses. Similarly, Marine courses may have value for Army students. Coordinate opportunities through TECOM.
3. **Doctrine and TTPs.** The Army publishes signal doctrine (ATP 6-02 series) that influences joint operations. Stay aware of Army doctrinal changes that affect how Marine communicators integrate in joint environments.
4. **Equipment commonality.** As both services field convergent networking capabilities (e.g., Integrated Tactical Network), coordination on MOS requirements and training standards becomes increasingly important.

### Relationship Management

The Army operates on a fundamentally different scale -- their Signal Branch manages tens of thousands of soldiers compared to your hundreds or low thousands of Marines. Their processes are more bureaucratic and slower to change. Be patient and recognize that they may not be able to match your coordination tempo.

That said, the relationship is valuable because the Army has already solved many problems you will encounter. Their experience with MOS restructuring, training pipeline management at scale, and retention programs can inform your approach even if the details differ.

### Key Venue

The Joint Communications Support Forum in September is the most efficient way to maintain this relationship and all other interservice communication coordination. Submit your travel request early -- it is always oversubscribed.

meta.relationship: Coordinates on joint billet management, interoperability requirements, and cross-service training opportunities. Annual joint conference in September.

entries.title: POF Branch Chief
entries.content:
## POF Branch Chief — Direct Supervisor

The Branch Chief at PP&O's Plans and Operations Future (POF) branch is your direct supervisor and the gatekeeper for everything that leaves the branch. Understanding their expectations, priorities, and communication preferences is essential to being effective in this billet.

### Daily Rhythm

- **0800 daily standup.** Branch Chief holds a 15-minute standup with all branch members. Format: each person gives a 60-second update on yesterday's actions, today's priorities, and any issues requiring the Branch Chief's attention. **Be concise and specific.** The Branch Chief values substance over volume.
- **End of day check-in.** Not a formal meeting, but the Branch Chief expects you to flag anything that changed during the day before you leave. A quick email or Teams message is sufficient.

### What the Branch Chief Expects from You

1. **No surprises.** The Branch Chief's worst day is when DirOps asks about something in your OccField that the Branch Chief hasn't heard about. If something is going sideways, inform the Branch Chief immediately. They would rather hear bad news from you first than from DirOps.
2. **Complete staff work.** When you bring a problem, bring a recommended solution. The Branch Chief will refine and adjust, but they expect you to have done the analysis.
3. **Accurate data.** Double-check your numbers before briefing. Getting caught with incorrect data undermines your credibility and the branch's reputation.
4. **Meet deadlines.** If a tasker has a due date, meet it or request an extension before the deadline. Never miss a deadline silently.

### Approval Authority

The Branch Chief is the approval authority for:
- Routine correspondence leaving the branch
- OccField manager travel requests
- Conference and symposium planning concepts
- Draft MOS Manual change packages (before routing to DirOps)
- Responses to data calls and information requests

The Branch Chief is **not** the approval authority for:
- MOS Manual changes (DirOps or higher)
- T/O changes above the minor threshold (DirOps)
- Budget requests (DirOps/DC PP&O)

### Communication Preferences

- Prefers face-to-face or phone for complex issues
- Email for routine coordination and documentation
- Teams for quick questions and status updates
- **Hates long emails.** Keep written communication under 150 words if possible. Attachments for details.

### Relationship Advice

The Branch Chief is a senior O-5 or junior O-6 who has been through the staff tour before. They understand your challenges but have limited patience for excuses. Build trust by consistently delivering quality work on time. Once trust is established, you will receive significantly more autonomy and top cover when you need it.

meta.relationship: Direct supervisor. Daily standup at 0800. Approves all OccField manager actions before routing to DirOps. Keep informed of any manning shortfalls or MOS health issues immediately — no surprises.

entries.title: DirOps, PP&O
entries.content:
## Director of Operations, PP&O — Senior Leadership

The DirOps is a one-star general officer (BGen) or senior colonel who oversees all operational planning functions within PP&O. You do not interact with DirOps daily, but when you do, the stakes are high and the expectations are absolute.

### Interaction Model

- **Weekly status brief.** DirOps receives a weekly rollup from each branch. Your input feeds through the Branch Chief. You will occasionally be pulled into the brief to answer specific questions about your OccField.
- **Quarterly MOS Health brief.** You present directly to DirOps quarterly. This is your highest-visibility recurring event. (See Process: Quarterly MOS Health Reporting.)
- **Decision briefs.** For major OccField decisions (MOS restructure, significant T/O changes, policy recommendations), you prepare the brief and present through the Branch Chief. DirOps makes the decision or routes to DC PP&O.

### Communication Protocol

**Critical rule: Never go directly to DirOps unless it is an emergency.** All routine communication flows through the Branch Chief. If the Branch Chief is unavailable and the issue is time-sensitive, you may approach DirOps directly, but inform the Branch Chief as soon as possible.

### What DirOps Values

1. **Brevity.** DirOps processes hundreds of data points daily. Your brief should be the clearest, most concise presentation of the week. Lead with the bottom line.
2. **Recommendations.** Never present a problem without a recommended course of action. DirOps will decide, but they expect you to have an informed opinion.
3. **Intellectual honesty.** If the data doesn't support your position, say so. DirOps has zero tolerance for cherry-picked statistics or spun narratives.
4. **Forward-looking analysis.** DirOps thinks in 2-5 year horizons. Tactical-level minutiae should be handled at the branch level. Bring strategic issues and trends.

### DirOps Decision Authority

- Approves MOS Manual change packages before routing to DC PP&O
- Approves T/O change recommendations above branch-chief threshold
- Directs studies and analyses on OccField health or force structure
- Can direct emergency manning actions (cross-deck orders, temporary duty adjustments)

### Preparing for DirOps Interaction

- Rehearse your brief with the Branch Chief first. Always.
- Prepare backup slides with detailed data. DirOps may not ask, but if they do, you need to have it ready.
- Know the context. DirOps sees your OccField in the context of all OccFields. Be prepared for comparison questions ("How does your fill rate compare to 03XX?").
- After any DirOps interaction, send a summary of action items and decisions to the Branch Chief within 2 hours.

meta.relationship: Reviews weekly status brief. Approves major decisions (MOS restructures, T/O changes over threshold). Brief through Branch Chief first — never go direct unless emergency.

entries.title: Annual MOS Manual Review Cycle
entries.content:
## Annual MOS Manual Review Cycle

The MOS Manual review is the cornerstone annual event for every OccField manager. This is your opportunity to make structural changes to your OccField that will shape force management for years. Missing the cycle means waiting another full year.

### Timeline

**October: Kickoff**
- PP&O issues the annual MOS Manual review data call. You receive a tasker to review all MOSs within your OccField and identify any changes needed.
- Pull current MOS health data from TFDW and M&RA. Identify any MOSs with persistent health issues (chronic over/under-staffing, high attrition, retention problems).
- Review symposium recommendations and fleet feedback for proposed changes.
- Begin drafting proposed changes. Each change requires a justification narrative.

**December: Draft Submission**
- Submit draft proposed changes to Branch Chief for internal review.
- Include: change description, justification, manning impact analysis, training pipeline impact, coordination status with M&RA and TECOM.
- Branch Chief reviews and provides feedback. Expect 1-2 revision cycles.

**February: Formal Staffing**
- Approved draft changes enter the formal staffing cycle.
- Route to M&RA, TECOM, and other affected stakeholders for concurrence.
- Respond to objections and negotiate compromises. **This is where most changes die.** Pre-coordination in October/November dramatically improves survival rate.
- CMC Staff review for major changes.

**April: Publication**
- All approved changes compiled into the annual MOS Manual update.
- Publications office formats and publishes.
- New manual is effective immediately upon publication, but implementation timelines for specific changes may vary.

### Preparation Checklist (Start in September)

- [ ] Pull TFDW inventory data for all MOSs in OccField
- [ ] Request retention data from M&RA
- [ ] Review training pipeline throughput data from TECOM
- [ ] Compile fleet feedback from symposium, unit visits, and correspondence
- [ ] Identify candidate changes and prioritize
- [ ] Pre-coordinate top priorities with M&RA monitor and TECOM POC
- [ ] Brief Branch Chief on planned submissions

### Key Lesson

**Start in September, not October.** The data call arrives in October but the analysis and pre-coordination should begin a month earlier. If you wait for the data call to start thinking about changes, you are already behind.

entries.title: POM Budget Submission
entries.content:
## POM Budget Submission — Program Objective Memorandum

The POM submission is the only mechanism to get new funding for your OccField's training and manning requirements. If you miss the POM window, you wait two years for the next cycle. This is a high-stakes, low-frequency event that requires meticulous preparation.

### What is the POM?

The Program Objective Memorandum is the Service-level budget document that translates strategy into funded programs for a 5-year period (Future Years Defense Program). Your role is to ensure that manpower, training, and equipment requirements for your OccField are included in the Marine Corps POM submission.

### Timeline

**November (T-3 months): Requirements Development**
- Review current unfunded requirements for your OccField: training seat shortfalls, instructor billet gaps, equipment modernization needs, new course development.
- Prioritize requirements based on operational impact. Not everything will be funded -- be ruthless in prioritization.
- Coordinate with TECOM on training-related requirements and with M&RA on manpower requirements.

**December-January: Justification Narratives**
- Write Program Change Proposals (PCPs) for each requirement. A PCP includes: description, justification, cost estimate (by fiscal year), risk if not funded, and alignment with Force Design guidance.
- **The narrative is everything.** Numbers alone don't get funded. Tie every dollar to operational capability. "Requesting $2.1M for 40 additional school seats to close a 30% throughput gap that will result in deploying battalions below 80% fill" is funded. "Requesting additional school seats" is not.

**February: Submission**
- Submit finalized PCPs through the Branch Chief to the PP&O POM coordinator.
- PP&O aggregates all requirements and conducts internal prioritization. You may be asked to defend your submissions or accept reduced funding.
- DirOps approves the PP&O POM input.

**March-May: Defense and Adjudication**
- The Marine Corps POM is developed at the Service level. PP&O, P&R, and other staff agencies negotiate the final content.
- Be prepared to defend your requirements in POM Issue Papers. Have your data and justification narratives ready for quick turnaround.

**June-July: POM Publication**
- The final POM is published. Review it to confirm your requirements survived the adjudication process.
- For requirements that were not funded, document them as unfunded requirements (UFRs) for possible Congressional add funding.

### Common Mistakes

- **Submitting late.** Late PCPs are dead PCPs. No exceptions.
- **Vague cost estimates.** "Approximately $1-2M" tells the comptroller nothing. Provide detailed, line-item cost breakdowns.
- **Not coordinating with TECOM.** If your training requirement is not also supported by TECOM in their POM input, it gets flagged as uncoordinated and deprioritized.

entries.title: Quarterly MOS Health Brief to DirOps
entries.content:
## Quarterly MOS Health Brief to DirOps

This is your highest-visibility recurring deliverable. Four times a year, you stand in front of a general officer and account for the health of your OccField. How well you prepare for this brief directly determines how much latitude and support you receive for the rest of the quarter.

### Schedule

- **Q1 Brief:** January (covering Oct-Dec data)
- **Q2 Brief:** April (covering Jan-Mar data)
- **Q3 Brief:** July (covering Apr-Jun data)
- **Q4 Brief:** October (covering Jul-Sep data and full FY rollup)

### Preparation Timeline (T-21 Days)

**Week 1 (T-21 to T-14):**
- Pull data from TFDW, MCTFS, and M&RA monitor spreadsheet
- Request retention data update from MMEA
- Request training throughput data from TECOM
- Begin building the slide deck using the standard PP&O MOS Health template

**Week 2 (T-14 to T-7):**
- Complete the analysis. Identify trends: what is improving, what is declining, what is stable
- Draft the executive summary and recommendations
- Prepare backup slides with detailed data for anticipated questions
- Pre-brief the Branch Chief. **This is not optional.** The Branch Chief will catch issues you missed and help refine the narrative.

**Week 3 (T-7 to brief day):**
- Incorporate Branch Chief feedback
- Finalize slides and distribute pre-read to DirOps exec (typically 48 hours prior)
- Rehearse. Time yourself. The brief window is 15 minutes for presentation plus 10 minutes for Q&A. Going over time is unprofessional.

### Brief Format (5 slides maximum)

1. **Executive Summary:** Overall health status (Green/Amber/Red by MOS), top 3 findings, recommendations
2. **Inventory Dashboard:** Fill percentages by MOS and grade, with trends
3. **Pipeline Status:** Training throughput, attrition, projected graduates
4. **Retention:** Reenlistment rates, comparison to targets, bonus effectiveness
5. **Issues and Actions:** Active issues with status, new issues identified, action items

### Post-Brief Actions

- Send summary of DirOps guidance and action items to Branch Chief within 2 hours
- Update the running issues tracker with any new direction
- Distribute sanitized version of the brief to stakeholders (M&RA monitor, TECOM POC) within 1 week

### DirOps Q&A Preparation

Anticipate these recurring questions:
- "How does your fill rate compare to Service average?"
- "What is your biggest risk for next quarter?"
- "If I gave you one more thing, what would it be?"
- "What is your retention bonus effectiveness rate?"

entries.title: OccField Specialist Symposium
entries.content:
## OccField Specialist Symposium — Annual Conference

The symposium is held annually in May. It is the single most effective mechanism for gathering fleet feedback, building OccField consensus, and generating the justification data you need for MOS Manual changes and POM submissions. Start planning 6 months out.

### Key Dates

- **November:** Submit venue reservation request and initial concept paper to Branch Chief
- **December:** Receive Branch Chief approval. Begin budget coordination with PP&O comptroller.
- **January:** Finalize attendee list. Send invitations via official PP&O correspondence.
- **February:** Collect RSVPs. Begin agenda development. Identify guest speakers.
- **March:** Finalize agenda. Distribute read-ahead materials (current MOS health data, pending issues, discussion topics).
- **April:** Final logistics coordination. Confirm A/V, printed materials, billeting, transportation.
- **May:** Execute symposium (typically 3 days, Tuesday-Thursday).
- **June:** Publish after-action report. Brief DirOps on outcomes and recommendations.

### Budget

Typical cost: $15K-$25K depending on attendee count and venue. Primary expenses:
- Room rental and A/V: $5K-$8K
- Materials and printing: $1K-$2K
- Per diem for non-local attendees (HQMC-funded): $8K-$12K
- Catering (working lunches): $2K-$4K

**Travel costs are borne by sending commands.** Include this in the invitation letter so commands can budget. Some commands will push back on travel costs -- have the Branch Chief engage their leadership if the symposium attendance is mandatory.

### Agenda Structure (3-Day Format)

**Day 1: State of the OccField**
- Opening remarks by Branch Chief or DirOps
- OccField Manager presents: MOS health overview, pipeline status, retention trends, pending policy changes
- Guest presentations: M&RA monitor (assignment landscape), TECOM (training pipeline updates)

**Day 2: Working Groups**
- Break into 4-5 working groups, each addressing a specific OccField issue
- Working groups produce recommendations with supporting data and implementation timelines
- OccField Manager floats between groups to ensure productive discussion

**Day 3: Outbriefs and Way Ahead**
- Each working group presents recommendations
- Open forum for issues not covered by working groups
- OccField Manager summarizes actions and assigns owners
- Closing remarks

### After-Action Requirements

Publish the symposium after-action report within 30 days. Route through Branch Chief to DirOps. The report becomes the justification backbone for the next MOS Manual review cycle and POM submissions. **Track every action item to completion** -- if recommendations die after the symposium, attendees will not take the next one seriously.

entries.title: TECOM Curriculum Review Board
entries.content:
## TECOM Curriculum Review Board — Quarterly

The Curriculum Review Board (CRB) is the formal mechanism for reviewing and updating course content for all MOS-producing courses in your OccField. Missing a CRB means waiting another quarter to address training quality issues.

### Schedule

Quarterly, typically held at TECOM headquarters in Quantico. Specific dates are set by TECOM and published at the beginning of each fiscal year.

- **Q1 CRB:** December/January
- **Q2 CRB:** March/April
- **Q3 CRB:** June/July
- **Q4 CRB:** September/October

### Preparation (T-14 Days)

1. **Collect fleet feedback.** Contact at least 3 operational units (battalion or equivalent) and ask: "How are your recent graduates performing? What skills are they lacking? What are they well-prepared for?" Document specific examples.
2. **Review attrition data.** Pull course attrition rates from TECOM. If attrition is above 15% for any course, prepare to discuss root causes.
3. **Review previous CRB action items.** Ensure you can report status on any items from the previous quarter's CRB.
4. **Prepare proposed changes.** If you have curriculum change recommendations, draft them in the TECOM-prescribed format: current state, proposed change, justification, resource impact.

### At the CRB

- **Attendees:** OccField Manager, TECOM Training Pipeline Manager, schoolhouse OIC and Chief Instructor, M&RA representative (sometimes), subject matter experts as needed.
- **Agenda:** Review of each course in the OccField -- throughput data, attrition analysis, curriculum updates, instructor staffing status, facility/equipment issues, fleet feedback.
- **Your role:** Present fleet perspective. Advocate for changes that align training output with operational requirements. Provide manning context that informs course capacity decisions.

### Decision Authority

- **Minor changes** (updated references, adjusted practical application percentages, modified lesson plans): Schoolhouse OIC can implement immediately.
- **Moderate changes** (adjusted course length by up to 1 week, new learning objectives, modified prerequisites): Requires TECOM Pipeline Manager approval.
- **Major changes** (course length change over 1 week, new course creation, fundamental POI restructure): Requires CG TECOM approval. Process takes 6-12 months.

### Post-CRB Actions

- Document all decisions and action items within 72 hours
- Distribute CRB summary to Branch Chief and M&RA monitor
- Track action items for the next CRB
- If major changes were approved, initiate any required coordination (M&RA for manning impact, PP&O for budget implications)

entries.title: Transfer Season Coordination (Apr-Jun)
entries.content:
## Transfer Season Coordination — April Through June

Transfer season is the busiest three months of the year for manning coordination. Approximately 60-70% of all PCS moves in your OccField happen between April and June. During this window, you will be in near-daily contact with the M&RA monitor, fielding requests from losing and gaining commands, and managing the inevitable disruptions.

### Preparation (Start in March)

1. **Review the assignment plan.** The monitor develops the assignment plan in January-February. By March, you should have reviewed it for any issues: billets being filled with wrong-grade Marines, key billets not prioritized, deploying units not receiving fills.
2. **Identify priority fills.** Work with the monitor to flag your top 10 most critical fills. These are typically: deploying unit shortfalls, instructor billets at TECOM, and key leadership positions.
3. **Coordinate with gaining commands.** Proactively contact the G-1s of commands receiving large numbers of your Marines. Confirm they are tracking inbound personnel and have reception plans.
4. **Align with schoolhouse graduation dates.** Coordinate with TECOM to ensure school graduates are available for PCS during the transfer window. Any graduation date that slips past June creates an off-cycle PCS that is harder to execute.

### During Transfer Season (April-June)

**Weekly coordination with monitor:**
- Review PCS order status: orders published, orders executed, orders delayed
- Identify and resolve mismatches: Marines ordered to units that don't need them, or critical billets not receiving fills
- Monitor divert requests: commands requesting to divert inbound Marines to different billets. Approve or deny based on OccField impact.

**Common Issues:**
- **Medical holds.** Marines with pending medical evaluations cannot PCS. This creates cascading shortfalls. Track medical hold numbers weekly and advocate for expedited processing when possible.
- **Dwell time waivers.** Marines below the minimum dwell time at their current duty station require a waiver to PCS. The monitor manages these but may need your support to justify exceptions.
- **School seat conflicts.** A Marine scheduled to PCS may also have an upcoming school date. Coordinate with the monitor to sequence school attendance and PCS appropriately.
- **Command retention requests.** Units losing Marines will sometimes request that fills be delayed or that specific Marines be retained. Evaluate these on a case-by-case basis, but default to the assignment plan unless there is a compelling operational reason.

### Post-Transfer Season (July)

- Compile transfer season metrics: fill plan execution rate, number of disruptions, fill percentage achieved
- Brief Branch Chief on results
- Feed lessons learned into the next year's planning cycle
- Begin pre-coordination with monitor for the next transfer season (it's never too early)

entries.title: Never Skip the Staffing Cycle
entries.content:
## Never Skip the Staffing Cycle

This is the single most important lesson I can pass on. In 2024, my predecessor attempted to expedite a MOS Manual change by going directly from the draft stage to publication, bypassing the formal M&RA and TECOM coordination. The rationale was that the change was "non-controversial" and "everyone already agreed informally." Here is what happened.

### What Happened

The change -- a modification to the prerequisites for MOS 0659 -- was drafted in October 2024 and submitted directly to the Publications office in November, skipping the standard M&RA and TECOM staffing cycle. The predecessor had verbal agreement from the monitor and the TECOM POC that the change was acceptable.

### Why It Failed

1. **CMC Staff rejected it.** During the CMC staff review (which is the final gate before publication), the change was flagged as "unconcurred" because neither M&RA nor TECOM had formal concurrence on file. The CMC Staff does not accept verbal agreements.
2. **M&RA non-concurred retroactively.** When M&RA was asked for formal concurrence after the fact, the monitor's boss (a branch head who had not been consulted) identified a manning impact that the monitor had not assessed. M&RA formally non-concurred.
3. **Back to square one.** The change had to restart from the beginning of the staffing cycle. Total time lost: 6 months. The change that was supposed to be in the FY25 MOS Manual update was pushed to FY26.

### The Lesson

The staffing cycle exists for a reason. Even when a change seems obvious and non-controversial:

- **Informal agreement is not concurrence.** A verbal "sure, that looks fine" from a working-level POC does not bind their organization. Only a formal concurrence memo signed by the appropriate authority counts.
- **Skipping steps creates enemies.** M&RA and TECOM felt blindsided. The working relationship was damaged for months. Rebuilding that trust cost far more time than the staffing cycle would have taken.
- **The shortcut is always longer.** 6 months lost vs. 8 weeks of normal staffing. The math speaks for itself.

### What to Do Instead

1. Pre-coordinate informally to build support and identify objections early
2. Then run the formal staffing cycle -- every step, every signature
3. If you need to accelerate the timeline, call each stakeholder personally and ask them to prioritize your package. That is the legitimate way to move faster.
4. Never, ever skip a step and assume it will be fine

entries.title: TFDW Data Lags Reality by 60-90 Days
entries.content:
## TFDW Data Lags Reality by 60-90 Days

The Total Force Data Warehouse (TFDW) is the authoritative system of record for Marine Corps manpower data. It is comprehensive, well-structured, and consistently 60-90 days behind reality. If you brief TFDW numbers as current inventory, you will be wrong, and someone in the room will know it.

### Why the Lag Exists

TFDW aggregates data from multiple source systems (MCTFS, TFSMS, unit diary entries). Each source system has its own update cadence, and the aggregation pipeline introduces additional delay. The result is that TFDW data typically reflects the force as it existed 2-3 months ago, not today.

### Specific Problem Areas

1. **Recent PCS moves.** A Marine who PCS'd last month may still show as assigned to their old unit in TFDW. This makes losing unit numbers look artificially high and gaining unit numbers look artificially low.
2. **Lateral moves and reclassification.** Marines who recently changed MOS will show in their old MOS for weeks or months after the change is effective.
3. **Accessions.** New graduates from MOS-producing courses take 30-60 days to appear in TFDW after graduation. This makes pipeline output look slower than it actually is.
4. **Losses.** Marines who EAS'd or were separated may continue to show in inventory for 30-45 days after departure.

### How to Get Accurate Numbers

1. **Cross-reference TFDW with the monitor's tracking spreadsheet.** The M&RA monitor maintains a live Excel file that is updated as PCS orders are published and executed. This is your most accurate source for current fill rates.
2. **Use MCTFS for spot checks.** MCTFS (Marine Corps Total Force System) is updated more frequently than TFDW for individual Marine status. Use it to verify specific data points.
3. **Contact the unit.** When accuracy matters (briefing DirOps, responding to Congressional inquiries), call the unit S-1 and ask for a head count. The unit knows who is actually standing in formation.

### Briefing Protocol

When presenting inventory data, always include the source and date:
- "TFDW data as of [date], supplemented by M&RA monitor data as of [date]"
- Never say "we have X Marines in this MOS" without qualifying when that number was pulled
- If asked for real-time numbers, say "I will confirm with the monitor and get back to you within 24 hours" rather than guessing

### The Trap

The most common trap for new OccField managers is pulling TFDW data the morning of a brief and presenting it as current. DirOps or the Branch Chief will ask a question that reveals the data is stale, and your credibility takes a hit. Always cross-reference. Always qualify your data source.

entries.title: The Monitor Relationship is Everything
entries.content:
## The Monitor Relationship is Everything

If there is one thing you take from this entire knowledge base, let it be this: your relationship with the M&RA monitor is the single most important factor in your success as an OccField manager. Manning data, policy changes, training pipelines -- all of it flows through or is influenced by the monitor. A good working relationship means problems get solved quickly, quietly, and in your favor. A bad one means every interaction is a battle.

### How to Build the Relationship

1. **Start with a face-to-face visit.** Within your first two weeks in the billet, go to Arlington and meet your monitor in person. Bring coffee. Spend an hour learning about their workload, their constraints, and their priorities. Do not bring a list of demands. This first meeting is about listening.

2. **Understand their world.** The monitor manages assignments for hundreds or thousands of Marines across your entire OccField and possibly others. They are constantly balancing competing demands from operational commands, individual Marine preferences, school dates, deployment cycles, and dwell time requirements. Your OccField is one of many competing priorities on their desk.

3. **Bring solutions, not just problems.** When you identify a manning issue, don't just drop it on the monitor and expect them to fix it. Come with a proposed solution: "I see a shortfall at 1st MarDiv for 0631s. I suggest we divert two Marines from the 29 Palms assignment pool. Here's why that works and doesn't create a problem elsewhere."

4. **Provide early warning.** If you know a retention problem is brewing, a training pipeline is about to choke, or a T/O change is coming that will affect their assignment plan, tell them before it hits their data. Monitors hate surprises even more than Branch Chiefs do.

5. **Be responsive.** When the monitor asks for your concurrence on a fill priority or an assignment question, respond within 24 hours. If you can't answer that fast, acknowledge receipt and give a timeline. Silence from the OccField manager makes the monitor's job harder.

6. **Protect the relationship during conflict.** You will disagree with the monitor on priorities. That is normal and expected. Handle disagreements professionally and at the working level. If you escalate to their boss or your boss without first trying to resolve directly, the relationship suffers permanently.

### What Happens When the Relationship Breaks Down

I have seen OccField managers alienate their monitor through arrogance, unresponsiveness, or going over their head. The result is predictable: your OccField becomes the last priority. Fills go to other fields first. Data requests take longer. Problems that could be solved with a phone call require formal correspondence. Your Marines pay the price.

### Maintenance

- Monthly face-to-face or video call (minimum)
- Quarterly in-person visit to Arlington
- Annual working lunch or dinner (off-clock relationship building)
- Always send a thank-you when they go above and beyond

entries.title: Budget Requests Die Without Justification Narratives
entries.content:
## Budget Requests Die Without Justification Narratives

Every OccField manager I have talked to has the same story: their first POM submission was rejected or deprioritized because it was a spreadsheet full of numbers with no narrative. The PP&O comptroller, the POM review board, and ultimately the decision-makers at the Service level are not going to fund a line item that says "40 additional school seats -- $2.1M." They need to know why, and they need it in terms they care about: operational readiness, deployment capability, and risk.

### What a Funded Narrative Looks Like

Here is an example of a justification narrative that survived the POM process:

> **Requirement:** 40 additional annual school seats for MOS 0612 (Tactical Switching Operator)
>
> **Cost:** $2.1M annually (instructor TAD, student per diem, equipment consumables)
>
> **Justification:** Current school seat allocation is 120/year against a requirement of 160, creating a persistent 30% training throughput gap. This gap directly causes deploying battalions to ship at 72-78% fill for 0612 billets. At current throughput, it will take 4.3 years to close the gap assuming zero additional losses. With this investment, the gap closes in 18 months. Without it, I MEF's next rotation deploys with a communications capability shortfall that cannot be mitigated by cross-training or augmentation.
>
> **Risk if not funded:** Deploying units remain below 80% fill threshold for communication billets through FY28. Operational commanders have rated this a top-3 readiness concern in two consecutive Quarterly Readiness Reports.

### Why Narratives Matter

1. **Decision-makers are not technical experts.** The POM review board includes general officers and SES civilians who manage across all domains. They need plain-language explanations of what capability they are buying and what risk they are accepting.
2. **Competing priorities.** Every dollar you request is a dollar that could go to infantry, aviation, or logistics. Your narrative must make the case that your requirement is at least as important as theirs.
3. **Audit trail.** Funded POM requirements are audited. The narrative becomes the official record of why the money was allocated.

### Tips for Writing Effective Narratives

- **Lead with operational impact**, not administrative convenience
- **Use specific numbers**: fill percentages, deployment timelines, capability gaps
- **Include the cost of inaction**: what happens if this is not funded?
- **Keep it to one page.** If you cannot justify the requirement in one page, you don't understand it well enough
